content.full_text:
Farn Polrrrcel PRACTTcEs CouurssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (9t6\ j22-0886
July 6,2006
David C. Laredo
City Attorney
City of Pacific Grove
505 Forest Avenue
Pacific Grove, California 93950
RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our FiIe No. I-06-105
Dear Mr. Laredo:
This letter is in response to your requestl on behalf of the City of pacific Grove
and the Pacific Grove Chamber of Commerce regarding the mass -uitirg provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").2 Please note that nothing in this letJer should be
construed to evaluate any conduct that has already taken place, such as the propriety of
any past mailings. (Regulation 18329.) In addition, this letter is based on the facts
presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as
the finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re oglesby (1975) I Fppc ops. 71.)
QUESTION
If the Pacific Grove Chamber of Commerce ("Chamber") receives a rental waiver
and funds from the city for "economic enhancement projects," is the Chamber newsletter
subject to the mass mailing restrictions of Government code $ g9001?
CONCLUSION
Under the facts you have presented, three of the four criteria set out in regulation
1 8901 (a) arb met. A mailing is prohibite d if alt the factors contained in this regulation
are satisfied. Thus, the prohibitions of section 89001 would apply to the Chamler
mailings if the fourth prong is met-that is, if the mailings are sent at public expense.
This would apply if: (1) any of the costs of distribution are paid for with public funds; or
(2) the costs of design, production and printing which exceed $50 are p*d for with city
I Informal assistance does not confer the immunity provided by a Commission opinion or formal
written advice. (Regulation 1 8329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)
' Govem-"nt Code sections 81000 - qtOt+. iommission regulations appearat Title 2, sections
1 8 1 09- 1 8997, of the California Code of Regulations.
r{g9e!


File No. I-06-105
Page No. 2
public funds, and the design, production, or printing is done with the intent of sending the
item other than as permitted under regulation 18901.
FACTS
The Pacific Grove Chamber of Commerce (the "Chamber") is a private
organization.
Every other month, the Chamber sends out a newsletter, "Business Rap IJp," to its
500 members and certain designated VIPs (city officials, important business contacts,
etc.). It is not distributed to all the residents of Pacific Grove. Chamber members are
participants who have paid an annual fee in exchange for certain benefits, such as
marketing, publicity, inclusion in special events and receipt of the newsletter.
The City of Pacific Grove (the "City") provides funding to the Chamber in two
ways. The Chamber receives money directly, as part of the City's budget for economic
enhancement projects, and indirectly, through a lease subsidy.
The Economic Advisory Committee ("EAC") is comprised of four members of
the community at-largq including one city council member, four commercial property
owners and four members of the business community. Each year, the EAC puts together
a request for funding of economic enhancement projects, based on suggestions from city
council and input from the public. This request is submitted by line item to the city
council for approval. Once the budget is approved, the Chamber seeks reimbursement
for project costs as they are completed.
Funds are expressly allocated to such items as web page marketing and brochure
reprints, and are not expressly directed to the publication and/or distribution of the
Chamber newsletter. Further, the City has not contracted with the Chamber to produce
the newsletter, and does not specifically direct the manner in which the Chamber chooses
to allocate funds for economic enhancement'
The City also provides funding to the Chamber, indirectly, in the form of a rent
waiver on a month-to-month lease. According to a lease between the City and the
Chamber, dated November 20,1991, the Chamber's monthly rental fee is waived "as an
in-kind contribution for the Chamber's activities to promote Pacific Grove and stimulate
local economy." The 1991 lease sets the monthly rental value at $600. The City does not
require the Chamber to produce, publish or distribute a newsletter as part of the waiver.
However, the city does not expressly limit or allocate the use of the savings for any
specific purposes in connection with promoting the City and stimulating the local
economy.


File No. I-06-105
Page No. 3
The Chamber's newsletter may include photos of City officials as well as
identification of them in captions, articles, special features andlor advertising. A copy of
the March 2006 Business Rap Up was included in your request for advice.
ANALYSIS
Mass Mailing Statute and Regulation
In 1988, section 89001 of the Act was added to provide that "no newsletter or
other mass mailing shall be sent at public expense." The Commission adopted regulation
18901 to clarify which mailings were perrnissible and which were prohibited under the
Act.
Under regulation 18901, a "mass mailing" is defined as more than two hundred
substantially similar tangible items delivered in a calendar month, by any means, to
recipients at their residence, place of employment, business, or post office box. (See also
Section 82041,.5 and Reg. 18435 [defining "mass mailing"].)
Regulation 18901(a) provides that a mailing is prohibited only if all of the
following apply:
(1) AnV item sent is delivered,by any means, to the recipient at his or
her residence, place of employment or business, or post office
box. For purposes of this subdivision (aXl), the item delivered to
the recipient must be a tangible item, such as a videotape, record,
or button, or a written document.
(2) The item sent either:
(A) Features an elected officer affiliated with the agency which
produces or sends the mailing, or
(B) Includes the name, office, photograph, or other reference to an
elected officer affiliated with the agency which produces or sends
the mailing, and is prepared or sent in cooperation, consultation,
coordination, or concert with the elected officer.
(3)(A) Any of the costs of distribution is paid for with public moneys;
or
(B) Costs of design, production, and printing exceeding $50.00 are
paid with public moneys, and the design, production, or printing is
done with the intent of sending the item other than as permitted by
this regulation.


File No. I-06-105
Page No. 4
(4) More than two hundred substantially similar items are sent, in a
single calendar month, excluding any item sent in response to an
unsolicited request and any item described in subdivision (b).
Thus, a mass mailing is prohibited if all of the factors set out in regulation
18901(a) are met. If the factors are not met, the mailing is not a "mass mailing" for
purposes of section 89001.
Delivery of a Tangible Item
Subdivision (a)(1) of regulation 18901, only restricts items that are mailed or
delivered, by any means, to a person's home, office or post office box. If items are set
out for the public to pick up on their own, or are handed out in a public area, the
restrictions of the regulation do not apply. You state in your letter that every other
month, the Chamber "sends out" a newsletter to more than 500 mernbers and certain
designated VIPs (city officials, important business contacts, etc.). You have not
described exactly how the newsletters are distributed. If the newsletters are mailed or
delivered, by any means, to a person's home, office or post office box, the criterion in
subdivision (aX1) would be met.
"Elected Officer"
The second criteria under regulation 18901, subdivision (a), indicates that if
reference is made to an "elected officer," a mailing could be prohibited. (Reg.
18e01(a)(2).)
Under the Act, an "elected officer" is any person who holds an elective office, has
been elected to an elective office but has not yet taken office, or who is appointed to fiIl a
vacarrt elective office. (Section 82020.) "Elective office" means any state, regional,
county, municipal, district, or judicial office which is filled at an election, as well as
membership on a county central committee of a qualified political party, and members
elected to the Board of Administration of the Public Employees' Retirement System
(PERS). (Section 82023.)
Since city council members are "elected officers" this criterion is met.3
References to Elected Officers Affiliated With the Agency
Subdivision(a)(2) of regulation 18901 describes two categories of mass mailings
regulated under the Act: (1) items that feature an elected officer affiliated with the agency
'Not", however, that candidates for city council who have not yet been elected to office, and are
not otherwise elected officers affiliated with the City, are not subject to the mass mailing restrictions of the
Act.


File No. I-06-105
Page No. 5
producing or sending the mailing, and (2) items that do not necessarily feature, but
include, a reference to an elected officer affiliated with the agency and are prepared or
sent in cooperation with that elected offrcer.a
If a mass mailing "features" an elected officer, it is subject to regulation 18901.
(See Reg. 18901, subd. (a)(2XA).) A mass mailing "features an elected officer" if it
includes the elected officer's photograph or signature, or singles out the elected officer by
the manner of display of his or her name or office in the layout of the document, such as
by headlines, captions, tlpe size, typeface, or tlpe color. (Reg. 18901, subd' (c)(2); see
also Reg. 18901, subd. (b)(9XB).)
It is anticipated that elected city officials will be featured in the Chamber
newsletter, therefore this criterion will also be met if the mailing is sent at public
expense.
Public Expense
Regulation 18901(a)(3) specifies that amass mailing is "sent at public expense"
within the meaning of Section 89001 if either: (t) any of the costs of distribution are
paid for with public moneys; or (2) more than $50 in public money are used to pay for the
iosts of design, production, and printing, and the design, production, or printing is done
with the intent of sending the item other than as permitted under the regulation.
We have advised that"amailing sent by a chamber of commerce is not 'sent at
public expense' for purposes of Section 89001 merely because the chamber receives
iome public moneys from a city, unless the public entity expressly funds the production
and distribution of the mailing sent by the chamber." [Emphasis added.] (See Curtis
Advice Letter,No. 4-94-053; Warner Advice Letter, No. I-88-392; Leslie Advice Letter,
No. 4-89-521.)
Therefore, the question your letter raises is whether the City expressly funds the
production and distribution of the newsletter either through: (1) the terms of its
agreement to provide a budget for economic enhancement projects; or (2) through its
lease agreement with the Chamber, which waives the Chamber's monthly rental fee of
approximately $600 in exchange for "the Chamber's activities to promote Pacific Grove
and stimulate local economy."
Your facts indicate that funds for the economic enhancement projects are
expressly allocated to discrete projects such as web page marketing and brochure
reprints, and are not expressly directed to the publication and/or distribution of the
Chamber newsletter.
o Pl"ase note that these two general restrictions have some exceptions found in subdivision (b) of
regulation 18901.


File No. I-06-105
Page No. 6
Furthermore, you state in your letter that the 1991 lease "does not require the
Chamber to produce, publish or distribute a newsletter as part of the waiver." Thus, we
conclude under these facts that the lease agreement between the City and the Chamber
(which provides indirect funding to the Chamber in the form of a rent waiver), does not
expressly provide funding for the production, publishing or distribution of the newsletter
Therefore, we conclude that based on the facts provided, not all the factors set out
in regulation 18901(a) would be met, and that the mass mailing restrictions of
Government Code $ 89001 would not apply to the newsletter that is produced, published
and distributed by the Chamber.
Note, however, if at any time the costs of distribution of the newsletter are
expressly funded by city funds, or the costs of design, production, and printing exceeding
$50 are expressly funded with public moneys, the publication of the newsletter would
constitute a prohibited mass mailing.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
2-r/&/4*
By: Emelyn Rodriguez
Counsel, Legal Division
Enclosure
